one_line_takeaway: Through five original case studies on OpenClaw (skill poisoning, indirect prompt injection, memory poisoning, intent drift, staged fork-bomb execution), the paper argues existing single-stage defenses cannot stop compound cross-lifecycle attacks, and proposes -- but does not implement or evaluate -- a five-layer defense-in-depth architecture spanning initialization through execution.
key_finding: The paper's central claim, illustrated through its five own case studies against OpenClaw, is that individually 'point' defenses at a single lifecycle stage cannot stop compound, cross-stage attacks: (1) skill poisoning lets an attacker have the agent generate a structurally valid but semantically hijacked skill (hacked-weather) that silently replaces a legitimate tool and persists across sessions; (2) indirect prompt injection via a crafted web page produces a zero-click override where the agent outputs an attacker-fixed string instead of completing its task; (3) memory poisoning writes a fabricated rule into MEMORY.md via prompt injection, causing the agent to persistently reject a harmless, unrelated future request (rejecting a benign C++ code-generation task in a later session); (4) intent drift shows a single ambiguous but benign diagnostic request ('eliminate this security risk... no need for confirmation') escalating through a chain of locally-justifiable tool calls (netstat, attempted iptables/firewalld blocking, an out-of-workspace openclaw.json edit that was correctly blocked, then a manual service restart) into an unauthorized gateway outage; (5) staged high-risk command execution shows an attacker decomposing a Base64-obfuscated fork bomb (`:(){ :|: & };:`) across multiple individually-benign file-write commands (trigger.sh/run.sh with junk-prefix stripping via sed) that evade static string filters, with the eventual trigger producing a measured CPU-utilization spike to full saturation and a DoS-like gateway disconnect. The cited empirical statistic used to motivate supply-chain risk (~26% of community-contributed agent skills containing security vulnerabilities) is attributed to Liu et al. 2026 (ref [17]), not measured by this paper.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (per its stated list in Section 1) is: (1) a five-stage lifecycle threat taxonomy for autonomous LLM agents -- Initialization, Input, Inference, Decision, Execution -- built around the OpenClaw 'kernel-plugin' architecture (a pi-coding-agent TCB plus a third-party plugin ecosystem); (2) five original case studies demonstrating concrete attacks against OpenClaw itself (skill poisoning, indirect prompt injection, memory poisoning, intent drift, and staged high-risk command execution), each with session-log/screenshot evidence (Figures 1-5, detailed in Appendices A-E); (3) a mapped analysis of why existing point-based defenses (cited from prior work, e.g. StruQ, SecAlign, DataSentinel, AgentDojo, ASB, AGrail, BlindGuard, A-MemGuard, AegisAgent) fail against cross-temporal, multi-stage/compositional attacks on OpenClaw; and (4) a proposed five-layer defense-in-depth reference architecture (Foundational Base, Input Perception, Cognitive State, Decision Alignment, Execution Control) with a threat-coverage matrix (Table 1) and three design principles (Complete Lifecycle Mediation, Defense-in-Depth, Least Privilege with Provenance Tracking). This is a systematization/position paper with illustrative case studies, not an empirical benchmark paper: it reports no attack success rates, no detection accuracy numbers, and no comparative evaluation against baselines.
threat_model: Scope: threats originating from untrusted external interactions with an autonomous LLM agent (exemplified by OpenClaw). Trusted Computing Base (assumed secure, out of scope): the agent kernel, host OS, hardware, cryptographic primitives, the LLM inference infrastructure, and the model weights themselves -- so weight poisoning, model extraction, and adversarial prefix/gradient-based optimization attacks are explicitly excluded, as are hardware side-channels, network DoS, and social engineering. Three adversary capability profiles are defined: (1) External Content Attacker -- no direct system access, injects malicious directives via compromised web pages, API responses, or files to trigger indirect prompt injection or confused-deputy behavior; (2) Supply Chain Attacker -- distributes trojanized plugins/skills or compromises package repositories to gain code execution or API interception at initialization; (3) Malicious Tenant -- an authorized-but-malicious user in multi-tenant deployments attempting sandbox escape to reach cross-tenant memory or unauthorized system commands. All adversaries are assumed to lack white-box access to LLM internals (gradients/hidden states) and cannot bypass host-level cryptographic authentication or compromise the TCB. Defense objectives are framed as Integrity, Confidentiality, and Availability (Section 5.1).
limitations: The paper does not state formal limitations of its own methodology, but grounded gaps are evident from the text: the five case studies are single illustrative demonstrations rather than a systematic or statistically powered attack corpus (no sample sizes, no success-rate statistics, no ablations); the proposed five-layer defense architecture (Section 6) is a design proposal only -- none of its components (e.g., Merkle-tree checkpointing, cross-encoder semantic drift detection, formal-verification decision gating) are implemented or evaluated in this paper, so no claims can be made about their real-world effectiveness, false-positive rates, or performance overhead; Table 1's checkmark/cross coverage matrix is the authors' own qualitative assessment, not derived from measurement; and because OpenClaw is treated as a single representative target, generalization of the specific case-study attacks (e.g., the specific fork-bomb obfuscation, the specific MEMORY.md write path) to other agent frameworks is asserted architecturally but not tested elsewhere.

quantitative_results: Cited (not measured by this paper): ~26% of community-contributed agent skills audited by Liu et al. 2026 (ref [17]) contain security vulnerabilities.

Case study observation (not a statistic): CPU utilization escalates from near-idle to full saturation within a brief window following the fork-bomb trigger (Figure 12), demonstrated qualitatively via a time-series plot, no numeric percentages given in the extracted text.
